rationale: The citing paper uses Diehl (2005) as specific prior evidence that consumers can search inappropriately even when decision aids are available. This evidence supports a core premise in the 2010 paper's theorizing: larger assortments do not necessarily translate into better realized preference match, which enables expectation disconfirmation to lower satisfaction. Although the citing paper does not directly reuse Diehl (2005)'s design, it relies on that finding in its conceptual setup rather than merely listing it as generic background.
relation_description: The 2010 paper treats Diehl (2005) as empirical grounding for the claim that more options and aided search can still produce poor search outcomes. That premise helps justify the 2010 paper's expectation-disconfirmation account of why larger assortments can reduce satisfaction.
